summary: Martin argues that while computer hardware has advanced by over twenty orders of magnitude in 40 years, the fundamental nature of software code has barely changed. He identifies three paradigms—Structured Programming (1968), Object Oriented Programming (1966), and Functional Programming (1957)—all discovered over 40 years ago. Each paradigm is characterized not by what it adds, but by what it takes away: constraints on direct transfer of control, indirect transfer of control, and assignment respectively. Martin suggests functional programming may become mandatory due to multi-core processors, as programs without assignment avoid concurrent update problems.
key_points: All three programming paradigms were discovered in a single decade over 40 years ago, and no new paradigms have emerged since
Each paradigm removes capabilities rather than adding them: structured programming removes goto, OOP removes function pointers, functional removes assignment
Modern languages have effectively enforced structured and OO paradigms by eliminating the alternatives
Functional programming may become necessary due to multi-core processors multiplying exponentially
A programmer from 1960 could still write code today after 24 hours of adjustment—the fundamental concepts haven't changed
money_quotes: Each of these paradigms took something away. None of them added any new capability. Each increased discipline and decreased capability.
Can we afford another paradigm? Is there anything left to take away?
We now play Angry Birds on our phones, which have the computing power of the freon cooled supercomputer monsters of the 70s.
We can barely get two concurrent threads to work properly, let alone 2^n literal processors.
There hasn't been a new paradigm in 40 years; so perhaps that's a good indication that there aren't any more to find.
key_insight: Programming paradigms don't give us new powers—they impose discipline by taking capabilities away, and this constraint-based evolution may have reached its natural limit.
